{{Short description|Treating large numbers of people for helminthiasis and schistosomiasis}}
{{Infobox medical intervention
| name         = Mass deworming
| image        = One Health 130423-N-IZ662-756.jpg
| caption      = Nurse giving a [[Anthelmintic|deworming tablet]] to a child in Kakute, [[Uganda]]
| alt          =
| pronounce    = 
| synonyms     =
| ICD10        =
| ICD9         =
| ICD9unlinked =
| CPT          =
| MeshID       =
| LOINC        =
| other_codes  =
| MedlinePlus  =
| eMedicine    =
}}
'''Mass deworming''', is one of the '''preventive chemotherapy''' tools,<ref name="WHO2006">{{cite book |author= WHO |year=2006|title= Preventive chemotherapy in human helminthiasis: coordinated use of anthelminthic drugs in control interventions: a manual for health professionals and programme managers| url= http://whqlibdoc.who.int/publications/2006/9241547103_eng.pdf |publisher= WHO Press, World Health Organization, Geneva, Switzerland |pages=1–61 |isbn= 92-4-154710-3}}</ref><ref name="Albonico2008">{{cite journal | vauthors = Albonico M, Allen H, Chitsulo L, Engels D, Gabrielli AF, Savioli L | title = Controlling soil-transmitted helminthiasis in pre-school-age children through preventive chemotherapy | journal = PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases | volume = 2 | issue = 3 | article-number = e126 | date = March 2008 | pmid = 18365031 | pmc = 2274864 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pntd.0000126 | doi-access = free }}</ref> used to treat large numbers of people, particularly children, for [[helminthiasis|worm infections]] notably [[soil-transmitted helminthiasis]], and [[schistosomiasis]] in areas with a high prevalence of these conditions.<ref name="Taylor2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Taylor-Robinson DC, Maayan N, Donegan S, Chaplin M, Garner P | title = Public health deworming programmes for soil-transmitted helminths in children living in endemic areas | journal = The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews | volume = 9 | issue = 9 | article-number = CD000371 | date = September 2019 | pmid = 31508807 | pmc = 6737502 | doi = 10.1002/14651858.CD000371.pub7 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Gabrielli AF, Montresor A, Chitsulo L, Engels D, Savioli L | title = Preventive chemotherapy in human helminthiasis: theoretical and operational aspects | journal = Transactions of the Royal Society of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene | volume = 105 | issue = 12 | pages = 683–93 | date = December 2011 | pmid = 22040463 | pmc = 5576527 | doi = 10.1016/j.trstmh.2011.08.013 }}</ref> It involves treating everyone – often all children who attend schools, using existing infrastructure to save money – rather than testing first and then only treating selectively. Serious side effects have not been reported when administering the medication to those without worms,<ref name="WHO2006" /><ref name="Albonico2008" /> and testing for the infection is many times more expensive than treating it. Therefore, for the same amount of money, mass deworming can treat more people more cost-effectively than selective deworming.<ref name=WHO1>{{Cite book |title = Helminth control in school-age children: a guide for managers of control programmes |edition=2nd |publisher=World Health Organization |year=2011 |isbn=978-92-4-154826-7 |page=vii |url=http://whqlibdoc.who.int/publications/2011/9789241548267_eng.pdf }}</ref> Mass deworming is one example of [[mass drug administration]].<ref name=Taylor2015 />

Mass deworming of children can be carried out by administering [[mebendazole]] and [[albendazole]] which are two types of [[anthelmintic]] drug.<ref name=WHO1 /> The cost of providing one tablet every six to twelve months per child (typical doses) is relatively low.<ref name=EAcost />

[[Soil-transmitted helminthiasis]] is the most prevalent [[Neglected tropical diseases|neglected tropical disease]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lo|first1=Nathan C.|last2=Heft-Neal|first2=Sam|last3=Coulibaly|first3=Jean T.|last4=Leonard|first4=Leslie|last5=Bendavid|first5=Eran|last6=Addiss|first6=David G.|date=2019-11-01|title=State of deworming coverage and equity in low-income and middle-income countries using household health surveys: a spatiotemporal cross-sectional study|journal=The Lancet Global Health|language=English|volume=7|issue=11|pages=e1511–e1520|doi=10.1016/S2214-109X(19)30413-9|pmc=7024997|issn=2214-109X|pmid=31558383}}</ref> Over 870 million children are at risk of parasitic worm infection.<ref>{{cite web|title=Soil Transmitted Helminths|url=https://www.who.int/mediacentre/factsheets/fs366/en |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120820003930/http://www.who.int/mediacentre/factsheets/fs366/en/ |archive-date=August 20, 2012 |publisher=WHO|access-date=28 July 2015}}</ref> Worm infections interfere with nutrient uptake, can lead to anemia, malnourishment and impaired mental and physical development, and pose a serious threat to children's health, education, and productivity. Infected children are often too sick or tired to concentrate at school, or to attend at all.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Baird S, Hicks JH, Kremer M, Miguel E | title = Worms at Work: Long-run Impacts of a Child Health Investment | journal = The Quarterly Journal of Economics | volume = 131 | issue = 4 | pages = 1637–1680 | date = November 2016 | pmid = 27818531 | pmc = 5094294 | doi = 10.1093/qje/qjw022 | url = http://emiguel.econ.berkeley.edu/assets/miguel_research/64/Worms-at-Work_2015-07-23.pdf | access-date = 2015-07-28 | archive-date = 2021-01-16 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20210116020318/http://emiguel.econ.berkeley.edu/assets/miguel_research/64/Worms-at-Work_2015-07-23.pdf }}</ref> In 2001, the [[World Health Assembly]] set a target for the [[World Health Organization]] (WHO) to treat 75% of school-aged children by 2010.<ref name=WHO1/>

Some [[non-governmental organizations]] support mass deworming, such as the [[Deworm the World Initiative]] (a project of the non-governmental organization [[Evidence Action]]), the END Fund (founded by [[Legatum|Legatum Foundation]] in 2012),<ref name="ft.com">{{Cite news|url=https://www.ft.com/content/aac0d518-0266-11e7-aa5b-6bb07f5c8e12|title=Philanthropy: the search for the best way to give|newspaper=Financial Times |date=April 2017|access-date=22 November 2019|language=en-GB}}</ref> the [[Schistosomiasis Control Initiative]], and [[Sightsavers]]. Because of the low cost of deworming children, large-scale implementation may provide wider benefits to society.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.givewell.org/charities/top-charities|title=GiveWell Top Charities|date=2018|website=Givewell|access-date=26 February 2019}}</ref>

{{TOC limit|3}}

== Background ==
[[File:US Navy 080923-N-7544A-274 Lt. Pandora Liptrot, a Navy Nurse Corps officer embarked aboard the amphibious assault ship USS Kearsarge (LHD 3) gives deworming medication to a child at the Center of the Grace of Good Samaritan Orp.jpg|thumb|A nurse giving deworming medication to a child]]
[[File:Hand washing as part of the Essential Health Care Package (EHCP) (3172335620).jpg|thumb|[[Hand washing]] and twice annual deworming is part of the Essential Health Care Package&nbsp;in the Philippines ("Fit for School" program).]]
Intestinal parasitic worms (most of them falling in the category of soil-transmitted helminths) affect approximately 1.5 billion people, according to WHO estimates,<ref>{{cite web |title=Soil-transmitted helminth infections |url=https://www.who.int/mediacentre/factsheets/fs366/en/ |publisher=WHO |access-date=14 May 2017|date=January 2017}}</ref> with 218 million needing preventive treatment for [[schistosoma]]-type worms in 2015.<ref>{{cite web |title=Schistosomiasis fact sheet |url=https://www.who.int/mediacentre/factsheets/fs115/en/ |publisher=WHO |access-date=14 May 2017|date=January 2017}}</ref>

The [[World Health Organization]] (WHO) recommends mass deworming of children who live in endemic areas, in order to reduce morbidity by reducing the overall worm burden.<ref>{{Cite web|title = WHO intestinal worms strategy |url =https://www.who.int/intestinal_worms/strategy/en/ |archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20070509152006/http://www.who.int/intestinal_worms/strategy/en/ |archive-date =May 9, 2007 |website = who.int |access-date = 2016-01-26}}</ref> The WHO advises that worm infections adversely affect nutritional status, impair cognitive processes, and can cause conditions such as intestinal obstruction or lesions in the urinary tract and liver. Periodic drug treatment is expected to bring about health benefits such as reduced micronutrient loss, reduced environmental contamination, improved nutritional status and cognitive function, and better school performance in certain circumstances.<ref name="Taylor2015" /><ref name=WHO1/>

In 2001, the [[World Health Assembly]] set a target for the WHO to treat 75 percent of school-aged children by 2010.<ref name=WHO1/> In 2014, over 396 million preschool and school-aged children were treated, corresponding to 47 percent of children at risk.<ref>{{Cite journal |title=Soil-transmitted helminthiases: number of children treated in 2014 |url=https://www.who.int/wer/2015/wer9051_52.pdf |journal=Weekly Epidemiological Record |issue=51/52 |volume=90 |pages=701–712 |date = 18 December 2015 |issn=0049-8114 |publisher=World Health Organization }}</ref>

== Methods ==

=== Pills ===
Deworming programmes for children usually administer an [[anthelmintic]] drug such as [[albendazole]] or [[mebendazole]] (or [[praziquantel]] in a weight based or height based dose for schistosomiasis). The treatment is given as a single dose in a pill formulation.<ref name="Taylor2015" /><ref name=WHO1 /> Other drugs used, though not approved by the WHO, include pyrantel pamoate, piperazine, piperazine citrate, tetrachloroethylene, and levamisole.<ref name="Taylor2015" /> In mass deworming programs, all children are given the medication, whether they are infected or not. In endemic areas, the deworming needs to be repeated regularly.<ref name="Taylor2015" /> The frequency of the treatment depends on the prevalence and severity of infection which is determined by periodic surveys but is usually required annually.<ref name=WHO1 />

=== Accompanying measures ===
To increase the benefits of mass deworming and to lower the rate of reinfection, accompanying measures of mass deworming programmes should include [[WASH|water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH)]] interventions.<ref name="HalesZiegelbauer2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Ziegelbauer K, Speich B, Mäusezahl D, Bos R, Keiser J, Utzinger J | title = Effect of sanitation on soil-transmitted helminth infection: systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = PLOS Medicine | volume = 9 | issue = 1 | article-number = e1001162 | date = January 2012 | pmid = 22291577 | pmc = 3265535 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pmed.1001162 | doi-access = free }}</ref> A good example for such a combined intervention is the Essential Health Care Program implemented by the Department of Education in the Philippines: This national programme combines twice annual deworming of school children with group [[Hand washing|handwashing]] with soap at set times of the day at the school premises.<ref name=":1">{{cite book|title=School Community Manual - Indonesia (formerly Manual for teachers), Fit for School.|date=2014|publisher=GIZ Fit for School, Philippines|isbn=978-3-95645-250-5|url=http://www.susana.org/en/resources/library/details/1513}}</ref><ref name=":0">UNICEF (2012) [http://www.unicef.org/wash/schools/files/Raising_Even_More_Clean_Hands_Web_17_October_2012(1).pdf Raising Even More Clehran Hands: Advancing Health, Learning and Equity through WASH in Schools] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304081726/http://www.unicef.org/wash/schools/files/Raising_Even_More_Clean_Hands_Web_17_October_2012(1).pdf |date=2016-03-04 }}, Joint Call to Action</ref> This so-called "Fit for School" approach has also been implemented in Indonesia in 2014.<ref name=":1"/>

== Health aspects ==

=== Evidence ===
* In 2015, a review in a [[World Bank]] journal concluded that evidence supports a benefit with respect to school attendance and long term income.<ref name = "Ahuja_2015">{{cite journal| vauthors = Ahuja A, Baird S, Hicks JH, Kremer M, Miguel E, Powers S |title=When Should Governments Subsidize Health? The Case of Mass Deworming|journal=The World Bank Economic Review|date=2015|volume=29|issue=suppl 1|pages=S9–S24|doi=10.1093/wber/lhv008|doi-access=free}}</ref>
* A [[Cochrane review]] updated in 2019 found that high-quality [[Evidence-based medicine|medical evidence]] on mass deworming of children did not support beneficial effect on school performance, body weight, cognition, and rates of anemia.<ref name="Taylor2015" /> However, it excluded a number of studies which showed positive long-term results as they did not meet the inclusion criteria of a pure control (for studies using the method of [[randomized controlled trials]] (RCT)). Supporters of mass deworming argue that these studies make a case for long-term benefits.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Ozier|first1=Owen | name-list-style = vanc |title=Exploiting Externalities to Estimate the Long-Term Effects of Early Childhood Deworming|journal=World Bank Group|date=Oct 2014|url=http://www-wds.worldbank.org/external/default/WDSContentServer/WDSP/IB/2014/10/01/000158349_20141001145539/Rendered/PDF/WPS7052.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=New deworming reanalyses and Cochrane review|url=http://blog.givewell.org/2015/07/24/new-deworming-reanalyses-and-cochrane-review/|website=The GiveWell Blog|access-date=12 August 2015|date=July 24, 2015}}</ref>
* A review in 2016 examining the effects of deworming on child weight included studies omitted from Cochrane. It also extracted additional data from included studies. This review concluded that in environments with greater than 20% prevalence, where the WHO recommends mass treatment, the estimated average weight gain per dollar expenditure from deworming MDA is more than 35 times that estimated from school feeding programs.<ref>{{Cite journal| vauthors = Croke K, Hicks JH, Hsu E, Kremer M, Miguel E |title=Does Mass Deworming Affect Child Nutrition? Meta-analysis, Cost-Effectiveness, and Statistical Power |journal=NBER Working Paper No. 22382 |date=July 2016 |doi=10.3386/w22382 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
* In 2017, a systematic review and meta-analysis re-examined available studies and concluded that mass deworming for soil-transmitted helminths had little effect. However, for schistosomiasis, mass deworming might have been effective for weight but probably was ineffective for height, cognition, and school attendance.<ref name="lancet2017">{{cite journal | vauthors = Welch VA, Ghogomu E, Hossain A, Awasthi S, Bhutta ZA, Cumberbatch C, Fletcher R, McGowan J, Krishnaratne S, Kristjansson E, Sohani S, Suresh S, Tugwell P, White H, Wells GA | display-authors = 6 | title = Mass deworming to improve developmental health and wellbeing of children in low-income and middle-income countries: a systematic review and network meta-analysis | journal = The Lancet. Global Health | volume = 5 | issue = 1 | pages = e40–e50 | date = January 2017 | pmid = 27955788 | doi = 10.1016/S2214-109X(16)30242-X | doi-access = free | hdl = 10393/39279 | hdl-access = free }}</ref>
* A 2019 meta-analysis from the [[Campbell Collaboration]] found that mass deworming of pregnant women reduced maternal anemia by 23%, but there was no evidence of other effects.<ref>{{Cite journal| vauthors = Salam RA, Cousens S, Welch V, Gaffey M, Middleton P, Makrides M, Arora P, Bhutta ZA | display-authors = 6 |date=2019|title=Mass deworming for soil-transmitted helminths and schistosomiasis among pregnant women: A systematic review and individual participant data meta-analysis|journal=Campbell Systematic Reviews|language=en|volume=15|issue=3|article-number=e1052|doi=10.1002/cl2.1052|issn=1891-1803|doi-access=free|pmc=8356523}}</ref>

=== Reinfection and resistance ===
Reinfection with worms may begin shortly after the pill has killed the intestinal worm population.<ref name="CooperJia2012">{{cite journal | vauthors = Jia TW, Melville S, Utzinger J, King CH, Zhou XN | title = Soil-transmitted helminth reinfection after drug treatment: a systematic review and meta-analysis | journal = PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases | volume = 6 | issue = 5 | article-number = e1621 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22590656 | pmc = 3348161 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pntd.0001621 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Regular re-treatment together with an increased focus on other aspects of [[WASH|water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH)]] reduces the rates of infection in areas where parasitic worms are endemic.<ref name="HalesZiegelbauer2012"/><ref name="CooperJia2012" />

Resistance of worms to anthelmintic drugs over time is a possibility.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Levecke B, Montresor A, Albonico M, Ame SM, Behnke JM, Bethony JM, Noumedem CD, Engels D, Guillard B, Kotze AC, Krolewiecki AJ, McCarthy JS, Mekonnen Z, Periago MV, Sopheak H, Tchuem-Tchuenté LA, Duong TT, Huong NT, Zeynudin A, Vercruysse J | display-authors = 6 | title = Assessment of anthelmintic efficacy of mebendazole in school children in six countries where soil-transmitted helminths are endemic | journal = PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases | volume = 8 | issue = 10 | article-number = e3204 | date = October 2014 | pmid = 25299391 | pmc = 4191962 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pntd.0003204 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==Costs==
Mass deworming has been determined to be cheap when calculated on a 'per child/per year'<ref name="EAcost">{{cite web |author=Williams, Katherine |title=How do we calculate the cost of deworming? |url=http://www.evidenceaction.org/blog-full/how-do-we-calculate-the-cost-of-deworming |website=Evidence Action |access-date=12 December 2016 |date=16 January 2015 }}</ref> or $/[[Disability-adjusted life year|DALY]]<ref>{{cite web|title=Cost-effectiveness in $/DALY for deworming interventions |url=http://www.givewell.org/international/technical/programs/deworming/cost-effectiveness |website=Givewell.org |access-date=30 July 2015 }}</ref> basis. Screening test to detect if a child is actually infected would be up to 12 times more expensive.<ref name = "Ahuja_2015"/>

The cost of treating a child for infection of soil transmitted helminths and schistosomes costs different amounts in different countries when administered as part of mass school-based deworming, but Evidence Action states that their recent programmes cost $0.56 or less per child per dose.<ref name="EAcost" /> This programme is recommended by [[Giving What We Can]] and the [[Copenhagen Consensus|Copenhagen Consensus Centre]] as one of the most efficient and cost-effective solutions. Modelling studies also suggest that deworming programmes are highly cost effective.<ref name="LoBogoch2015">{{cite journal | vauthors = Lo NC, Bogoch II, Blackburn BG, Raso G, N'Goran EK, Coulibaly JT, Becker SL, Abrams HB, Utzinger J, Andrews JR | display-authors = 6 | title = Comparison of community-wide, integrated mass drug administration strategies for schistosomiasis and soil-transmitted helminthiasis: a cost-effectiveness modelling study | journal = The Lancet. Global Health | volume = 3 | issue = 10 | pages = e629–38 | date = October 2015 | pmid = 26385302 | doi = 10.1016/S2214-109X(15)00047-9 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==National deworming programmes==
National deworming programmes target children of school age, which the WHO defines as being between 5 and 14 years of age.<ref name=WHO1 /> By 2015, the total global number estimated to be in deworming programmes was 495 million<ref>{{cite web |title=Deworming campaign improves child health, school attendance in Rwanda |url=https://www.who.int/features/2015/rwanda-deworming-campaign/en/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150718084640/http://www.who.int/features/2015/rwanda-deworming-campaign/en/ |archive-date=July 18, 2015 |website=WHO.int |date=2015}}</ref> and national deworming programs had been started in a number of countries. The world's largest deworming programme was started in 2015 in India, with an aim to target 240 million children at risk for parasitic worms.<ref name="India">{{cite web |title=World's largest deworming program in India to start |url=http://www.evidenceaction.org/blog-full/largest-deworming-program-in-india-to-start-with-support-from-evidence-action |website=Evidence Action |access-date=28 July 2015 |date=2015}}</ref>

National deworming programmes listed by country in alphabetical order:
* Burundi: around 2 million children received two doses of medication in 2014.<ref name="SCI" />
* Cameroon: began a deworming programme in 2006, it expanded to target 4 million children.<ref>{{cite web|title=Scaling Cameroon's deworming program to the national level|url=http://www.childrenwithoutworms.org/quarterly-dose-articles/scaling-cameroons-deworming-program-to-the-national-level|website=Children Without Worms|access-date=29 July 2015|date=2012}}{{Dead link|date=February 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>
* Côte d'Ivoire: more than 1.4 million children treated in 2014<ref name="SCI">{{cite web |title=SCI Summary sheet of treatments instigated and overseen by SC |url=http://www.givewell.org/files/DWDA%202009/SCI/SCI%20country%20table.xls |website=Givewell |access-date=30 July 2015 |date=2014}}</ref>
* Central African Republic: began a deworming programme in 2015 aiming to target 250,000 children.<ref>{{cite web |title=The Central African Republic: going beyond providing food as part of our school meal programme |url=https://www.wfp.org/stories/car-school-meals-programmes-go-beyond-providing-food |website=World Food Programme |access-date=29 July 2015 |date=2015 |archive-date=26 July 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150726155712/http://www.wfp.org/stories/car-school-meals-programmes-go-beyond-providing-food }}</ref>
* Democratic Republic of Congo: began a deworming campaign in 2009 aiming to target 12.5 million children.<ref>{{cite news |title=DRC: National deworming campaign under way |url=http://www.irinnews.org/report/84863/drc-national-deworming-campaign-under-way |access-date=29 July 2015 |agency=IRIN |date=2009}}</ref>
* Ethiopia: announced it would begin a national deworming programme in 2015.<ref>{{cite news|title=Ethiopia launches school program to treat parasitic worms |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-ethiopia-health-idUSKBN0KI0P620150109 |access-date=29 July 2015 |work=Reuters }}</ref> following an estimated 6.8 million children treated in 2014.<ref name="SCI" />
* Gambia: began a deworming programme in 2010, by 2013 it was targeting 1.6 million children.<ref>{{cite web |title=Gambia SHN success story |url=http://schoolsandhealth.org/gambia-shn-success-story|website=Schools and Health|access-date=29 July 2015}}</ref>
* Kenya: began a deworming programme in 2009 of all children in 45 districts of high density STH infections.<ref name="Kabaka">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kabaka S, Kisia CW |title=National deworming program - Kenya's experience |journal=World Conference on the Social Determinants of Health |date=2011 |url=https://www.who.int/sdhconference/resources/draft_background_paper22_kenya.pdf }}</ref> By 2014, the programme had expanded to target 6 million children.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kenya national deworming programme |url=https://ciff.org/grant-portfolio/kenya-national-deworming-programme/ |website=Children's Investment Fund Foundation |access-date=29 July 2015 |archive-date=20 December 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220114014/https://ciff.org/grant-portfolio/kenya-national-deworming-programme/ }}</ref>
* India: announced a deworming programme in 2015 which aimed to treat 240 million children.<ref name="India" />
* Liberia: more than 600,000 children treated in 2014, but delayed due to ebola<ref name="SCI" />
* Madagascar: began a deworming programme in 2012 aiming to target all of the children in the country, more than 5 million in total.<ref>{{cite web |title=Deworming in Madagascar: the power of partnerships |url=http://www.childrenwithoutworms.org/quarterly-dose-articles/deworming-in-madagascar-the-power-of-partnerships |website=Children Without Worms |access-date=29 July 2015 |date=2012 }}{{Dead link|date=February 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>
* Malawi: around 2 million children targeted in a deworming programme in 2011.<ref>{{cite web |title=Child Health Week reaches more than two million children in Malawi |url=http://www.unicef.org/malawi/media_8350.html |website=UNICEF Malawi |access-date=29 July 2015 |date=2011 |archive-date=4 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304071200/http://www.unicef.org/malawi/media_8350.html }}</ref>
* Mozambique: began a deworming programme in 2007 when nearly 500,000 children were treated, by 2014 around 5 million were targeted.<ref>{{cite web |title=Schistosomasis control initiative - Mozambique |url=http://www3.imperial.ac.uk/schisto/wherewework/mozambique/mozambiquestrategy|website=Imperial College|access-date=29 July 2015}}</ref>
* Niger: began a deworming programme in 2004,<ref name="BrookerLeslie2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Leslie J, Garba A, Oliva EB, Barkire A, Tinni AA, Djibo A, Mounkaila I, Fenwick A | display-authors = 6 | title = Schistosomiasis and soil-transmitted helminth control in Niger: cost effectiveness of school based and community distributed mass drug administration [corrected] | journal = PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases | volume = 5 | issue = 10 | article-number = e1326 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 22022622 | pmc = 3191121 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pntd.0001326 | doi-access = free }}</ref> in 2014 more than 1.3 million children.<ref name="SCI" />
* Senegal: more than 500,000 children treated in 2013.<ref name="SCI" />
* Sierra Leone: 1.1 million school children received two doses of medication in 2011<ref>{{cite web |title=Sierra Leone |url=http://65.181.121.234/working-worldwide/africa/sierra-leone/#STH |website=Helen Keller International |access-date=29 July 2015 |archive-date=20 December 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220130844/http://65.181.121.234/working-worldwide/africa/sierra-leone/#STH }}</ref>
* Tanzania: more than 960,000 children treated in 2014.<ref name="SCI" />
* Uganda: more than 500,000 received a biannual treatment in 2014.<ref name="SCI" />
* Yemen: more than 2.5 million children treated in 2014, but programme on hold in 2015 due to political unrest.<ref name="SCI" />
* Zambia: more than 90,000 children treated in 2014.<ref name="SCI" />
* Zanzibar: almost 1.7 million children treated in 2014.<ref name="SCI" />

=== Acceptance ===
Deworming programmes are widely accepted, although there have been some reports of parents refusing to allow their children to receive medication due to fears of illness, such as those reported in the media in the Philippines.<ref name="Garcia" />
One survey in the Philippines reported that some parents will not allow their children to receive deworming tablets, while the majority would.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Parikh DS, Totañes FI, Tuliao AH, Ciro RN, Macatangay BJ, Belizario VY | title = Knowledge, attitudes and practices among parents and teachers about soil-transmitted helminthiasis control programs for school children in Guimaras, Philippines | journal = The Southeast Asian Journal of Tropical Medicine and Public Health | volume = 44 | issue = 5 | pages = 744–52 | date = September 2013 | pmid = 24437309 | url = http://www.tm.mahidol.ac.th/seameo/2013-44-5-full/03-5797-8.pdf }}</ref> Another in rural China found that skepticism and local rumors about the deworming programme could affect the uptake of medication.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Lu L, Liu C, Zhang L, Medina A, Smith S, Rozelle S | title = Gut instincts: knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding soil-transmitted helminths in rural China | journal = PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases | volume = 9 | issue = 3 | article-number = e0003643 | date = March 2015 | pmid = 25807188 | pmc = 4373855 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pntd.0003643 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==Other actors==

===UN agencies and NGOs===
The UN is involved in mass deworming programmes via the [[World Food Programme]],<ref name="NGOdewinv">{{cite web|author1=Global NGO Deworming Inventory 2010 |title=2010 Global NGO Deworming Inventory summary report: deworming programs by country |url=http://storage.ugal.com/5115/deworming-programs-by-country-2010-3.1.12.pdf |website=deworminginventory.org |date=2011}}</ref> [[UNICEF]]<ref name="Kabaka" /> and [[World Health Organization]].<ref name="Kabaka" />

NGOs involved in deworming advocacy or delivery include: the [[Schistosomiasis Control Initiative]], the [[Deworm the World Initiative]] from Evidence Action, Goods For Good, [[Save the Children]], [[Counterpart International]], [[Helen Keller International]], the [[Carter Center]], Inmed Partnerships for Children, [[Operation Blessing International]], and [[Children Without Worms]].<ref name="NGOdewinv" />

===Pharmaceutical companies===
[[Biotechnology]] companies in the developing world have targeted [[neglected tropical diseases]] – which many helminth infections are classified as – and [[mass drug administration]] due to a need to improve global health.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Frew SE, Liu VY, Singer PA | title = A business plan to help the 'global South' in its fight against neglected diseases | journal = Health Affairs | volume = 28 | issue = 6 | pages = 1760–73 | year = 2009 | pmid = 19887417 | doi = 10.1377/hlthaff.28.6.1760 | url = http://siteresources.worldbank.org/EDUCATION/Resources/278200-1099079877269/547664-1099079975330/Peter_Singer_Neglected_Tropical_Diseases.pdf }}</ref><ref name="Keenan">{{cite journal | vauthors = Keenan JD, Hotez PJ, Amza A, Stoller NE, Gaynor BD, Porco TC, Lietman TM | title = Elimination and eradication of neglected tropical diseases with mass drug administrations: a survey of experts | journal = PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases | volume = 7 | issue = 12 | article-number = e2562 | year = 2013 | pmid = 24340111 | pmc = 3855072 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pntd.0002562 | doi-access = free }} {{open access}}</ref>

For example, in 2012 [[Johnson & Johnson]] pledged 200 million deworming tablets per year.<ref name=Fenwick>{{cite journal | vauthors = Fenwick A | title = The global burden of neglected tropical diseases | journal = Public Health | volume = 126 | issue = 3 | pages = 233–236 | date = March 2012 | pmid = 22325616 | doi = 10.1016/j.puhe.2011.11.015 }}</ref>

==Examples==

===Nicaragua===

The national deworming programme was established as a partnership between the [[Pan American Health Organization]] – which serves as the Regional Office of the WHO, the [[Global Network for Neglected Tropical Diseases]], the [[Inter-American Development Bank]], Nicaraguan Government ministries, International NGOs and the main donor [[Children Without Worms]].<ref name=lucien>{{cite journal|vauthors=Lucien F, Janus CB|title=Worms and WASH(ED) Nicaragua|date=2011|publisher=The George Washington University|url=http://storage.ugal.com/5116/nicaragua-case-studyworms-and-washed-july-2012.pdf|access-date=30 July 2015|archive-date=4 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304230033/http://storage.ugal.com/5116/nicaragua-case-studyworms-and-washed-july-2012.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Children Without Worms is a public-private partnership between [[The Task Force for Global Health]] and [[Johnson & Johnson]], who donated the mebendazole medication.<ref name=lucien /> Intestinal helminths are a major problem in Nicaragua with 73% of rural households lacking clean drinking water and 73% lacking sanitation.<ref name=lucien />

Since 2009, drug donations from Johnson & Johnson have enabled annual deworming of school-aged children and medication supplied by other NGOs has enabled deworming of pre-school children, although questions were raised as to whether the frequency should have been increased.<ref name=lucien />

===Burundi===

In 2007, Burundi undertook a parasitological survey across its population which demonstrated that intestinal schistosomiasis was highly focalised in areas close to water and soil-transmitted helminthiases, including hook worm, was more widely distributed amongst its people. The report demonstrated the suitability of a mass drug administration for the entire population.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite journal | vauthors = Ndayishimiye O, Ortu G, Soares Magalhaes RJ, Clements A, Willems J, Whitton J, Lancaster W, Hopkins A, Fenwick A | display-authors = 6 | title = Control of neglected tropical diseases in Burundi: partnerships, achievements, challenges, and lessons learned after four years of programme implementation | language = en-GB | journal = PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases | volume = 8 | issue = 5 | article-number = e2684 | date = May 2014 | pmid = 24785993 | pmc = 4006741 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pntd.0002684 | doi-access = free }}</ref> The Schistosomiasis Control Initiative had developed an approach which would cost 50 US cents per person per year, and was piloted in Burundi and Rwanda in 2007 with a $9 million investment from the Legatum Foundation, along with support from various other government donors, philanthropists and pharmaceutical companies, over a four-year period.<ref name="ft.com"/><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.afro.who.int/news/national-programme-managers-and-partners-meet-take-stock-progress-african-region|title=National Programme Managers and Partners Meet to Take Stock of Progress in the African Region|date=July 2018|access-date=22 November 2019|language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="ReferenceA"/>

=== Philippines ===

====Soil Transmitted Helminth Control Program run by the Department of Health====

The Philippines Soil Transmitted Helminth Control Program is run by the Department of Health and implemented by the Department of Education in the Philippines.<ref name=philsth>{{cite web|title=Soil Transmitted Helminth Control Program|url=http://www.doh.gov.ph/content/soil-transmitted-helminth-control-program.html|website=Republic of the Philippines Department of Health|access-date=31 July 2015|archive-date=4 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150104172715/http://www.doh.gov.ph/content/soil-transmitted-helminth-control-program.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> It is a partnership with the [[WHO]], [[University of the Philippines Manila]], [[UNICEF]], [[World Vision International]], [[Feed the Children]], [[Helen Keller International]], [[Plan (aid organisation)|Plan International]] and [[Save the Children]].<ref name=philsth /> It involves giving all children doses of Albendazole or Mebendazole.<ref name=philsth />

However, in 2013 the Director of the National Institute of Health in the Philippines questioned the effectiveness of the programme because it only covered only 20% of affected children with an infection rate of 44%.<ref name=Tubeza>{{cite news|last1=Tubeza|first1=Philip| name-list-style = vanc |title=Gov deworming programme in public schools not very effective|url=http://newsinfo.inquirer.net/365399/govt-deworming-program-in-public-schools-not-very-effective-says-health-exec|access-date=31 July 2015|agency=Philippine Daily Inquirer|date=2013}}</ref> By 2015, 16 million children were targeted in the deworming programme.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Crisostomo|first1=Sheila| name-list-style = vanc |title=DOH targets deworming of 16M students|url=http://www.philstar.com/headlines/2015/07/26/1481053/doh-targets-deworming-16-m-students|access-date=31 July 2015|agency=philststar|date=July 2015}}</ref> with some media claims that some of the medication was found to be expired.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Geronimo|first1=Jee| name-list-style = vanc |title=DOH looks into 'expired' deworming medicine claim|url=http://www.rappler.com/nation/101010-doh-probe-expired-deworming-medicine-zamboanga|access-date=31 July 2015|agency=Rappler|date=2015}}</ref> Government officials later denied this.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Jocson|first1=Liza|name-list-style=vanc|title=DOH confirms deworming medicine not expired|url=http://cnnphilippines.com/regional/2015/07/30/DOH-confirms-deworming-medicine-not-expired.html|access-date=31 July 2015|agency=CNN Philippines|date=2015|archive-date=12 August 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150812041301/http://cnnphilippines.com/regional/2015/07/30/DOH-confirms-deworming-medicine-not-expired.html}}</ref> Other national press reports in July 2015 stated that a small number of children had been admitted to hospital due to an "adverse effect" of the deworming medication.<ref name=Garcia>{{cite news|last1=Garcia|first1=Bong| name-list-style = vanc |title=Deworming tablet downs hundreds of schoolchildren|url=http://www.sunstar.com.ph/zamboanga/local-news/2015/07/29/deworming-tablet-downs-hundreds-schoolchildren-421671|access-date=31 July 2015|agency=Sun Star|date=July 2015}}</ref>

====Essential Health Care Program implemented by the Philippine Department of Education====
Successful deworming and positive health outcomes were also achieved by the Essential Health Care Program implemented by the Department of Education in the Philippines. This national programme includes giving school children deworming drugs twice a year, as well as group [[Hand washing|handwashing]] with soap and brushing teeth daily with [[fluoride]] toothpaste as a group activity at set times of the day at the school premises.<ref name=":1"/> In 2012 [[UNICEF]] described it as an "outstanding example of at scale action to promote children's health and education".<ref name=":0"/>

===United States===
Public health campaigns to reduce helminth infections in the U.S. may be traced as far back as 1910, when the [[Rockefeller Foundation]] began the fight against hookworm – the so-called "germ of laziness" – which was found to infect 40% of children in the American South.<ref name="BundyWalson2013">{{cite journal |author2-link=Judd Walson | vauthors = Bundy DA, Walson JL, Watkins KL | title = Worms, wisdom, and wealth: why deworming can make economic sense | journal = Trends in Parasitology | volume = 29 | issue = 3 | pages = 142–48 | date = March 2013 | pmid = 23332661 | doi = 10.1016/j.pt.2012.12.003 }}</ref><ref name="Wat2">{{cite journal | vauthors = Watkins WE, Pollitt E | title = "Stupidity or worms": do intestinal worms impair mental performance? | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 121 | issue = 2 | pages = 171–91 | date = March 1997 | pmid = 9100486 | doi = 10.1037/0033-2909.121.2.171 }}</ref>
Records of the programme suggest that it led to increased school enrollment and attendance for children, and improved literacy and income for adults who were treated as children.<ref name="BundyWalson2013" /><ref name="Ble2">{{cite journal | vauthors = Bleakley H | title = Disease and Development: Evidence from Hookworm Eradication in the American South | journal = The Quarterly Journal of Economics | volume = 122 | issue = 1 | pages = 73–117 | year = 2007 | pmid = 24146438 | pmc = 3800113 | doi = 10.1162/qjec.121.1.73 }}
</ref>
This campaign was enthusiastically received by educators throughout the region; as one [[Virginia]]n school observed: "children who were listless and dull are now active and alert; children who could not study a year ago are not only studying now, but are finding joy in learning... for the first time in their lives their cheeks show the glow of health."<ref name="Ble2" /> From [[Louisiana]], a grateful school board added: "As a result of your treatment&nbsp;... their lessons are not so hard for them, they pay better attention in class and they have more energy&nbsp;... In short, we have here in our school-rooms today about 120 bright, rosy-faced children, whereas had you not been sent here to treat them we would have had that many pale-faced, stupid children."<ref name="Ble2" />

Military personnel returning from the [[Second World War]] were found to be bringing intestinal worms back to the United States, so in 1947 the [[American Society of Parasitologists]] called for increased attention on deworming.<ref name="BundyWalson2013" />

==History==
{{For timeline|Timeline of deworming}}

Following [[World War II]], the [[World Health Organization]] "has been the principal body concerned with the international support of research and control programmes" of schistosomiasis.<ref name="sandbach">{{cite journal | vauthors = Sandbach FR | title = The history of schistosomiasis research and policy for its control | journal = Medical History | volume = 20 | issue = 3 | pages = 259–75 | date = July 1976 | pmid = 792584 | pmc = 1081781 | doi = 10.1017/s0025727300022663 }}</ref>{{rp|266}}

In 2001, the [[World Health Assembly]] declared the goal of 75% of schoolchildren in endemic areas receiving deworming treatment.<ref name="bundy">{{cite web |url=http://www.londonntd.org/sites/www.londonntd.org/files/content/Worms,%20Wisdom%20and%20Wealth.pdf |title=Worms, wisdom, and wealth: why deworming can make economic sense |access-date=April 16, 2016 |date=2013 |author1=Donald A.P. Bundy |author2=Judd L. Walson |author3=Kristie L. Watkins |archive-date=June 24, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160624202448/http://www.londonntd.org/sites/www.londonntd.org/files/content/Worms,%20Wisdom%20and%20Wealth.pdf }}</ref>{{rp|2}}
